identity.title: Other Justice, Public Order, and Safety Activities

lens.included_activities: U.S. government establishments engaged in general administration of public order and safety, public-safety statistics, consumer product safety administration, emergency planning, and disaster preparedness or management, assessed under the fixed lower-middle-market external-service lens.
lens.excluded_activities: Courts, police protection, government legal counsel and prosecution, correctional institutions, parole and probation offices, pardon boards, fire protection, private security services, private business-continuity consulting, captive internal units, shells, and non-control financing vehicles.
lens.customer_and_revenue_model: The in-code establishments deliver public administration funded through appropriations, taxes, grants, and intergovernmental transfers rather than recurring commercial fees paid by external customers. Private contractors may supply agencies, but their activity is classified outside this government-only code.

a.bridge: BLS describes emergency-management-director tasks and GAO documents AI use across federal agencies, not a wage-weighted task share for NAICS 922190. I map information gathering, hazard research, plan drafting, damage-assessment support, grant documentation, statistics, communications, knowledge retrieval, and program reporting into exposed work while retaining interagency negotiation, public accountability, leadership, exercises, real-time command, and high-stakes judgment; the emergency-management occupation may overstate mission work and understate clerical or statistical exposure elsewhere in the code.
a.rationale: The code contains planning, statistics, analysis, grant, and administrative workflows that are text- and data-intensive. GAO reports that agencies use generative AI for summarization, information access, reporting, program tracking, and emergency management. Human leadership and accountable decisions during emergencies keep a large share outside substitution.
a.change_evidence: A wage-weighted task inventory for federal, state, and local establishments coded 922190, with time allocation across planning, statistics, grants, communications, exercises, coordination, command, and administration and an audit of automation already in use.
caveats: The frozen labor-share input l is missing because government activity has no comparable receipts denominator.
Emergency management directors are only one occupation within a broad residual public-administration code.
Federal AI-use evidence may not represent state and local agencies or distinguish labor substitution from augmentation.

rho.bridge: GAO measures reported federal AI use cases, stages, and adoption barriers rather than five-year implementation of exposed work in all 922190 establishments. I infer implementation from rapid growth in reported use and relevant mission categories, then discount for procurement, authorization, fragmented legacy systems, technical staffing, privacy, cybersecurity, bias, hallucinations, records rules, and human-review requirements; federal activity may overstate capability in small local offices.
rho.rationale: Government adoption is underway, especially for mission-support tasks, but many reported use cases remain in initiation, acquisition, or development. High-stakes public-safety decisions require governance, data quality, monitoring, and auditability, while budgets and technical staff constrain rollout. Routine drafting and retrieval should implement faster than operational decision support.
rho.change_evidence: A longitudinal inventory of state, local, and federal 922190 deployments reporting workflow coverage, production status, user adoption, procurement time, accuracy, human-review burden, incident rates, hours avoided, and systems retired.
caveats: Reported use-case counts do not measure workforce coverage, quality, or realized hours avoided.
Federal acquisition and governance constraints differ from those of state and local governments.
Emergency and safety contexts make false outputs more costly than in ordinary back-office work.

e.rationale: The official NAICS definition limits this industry to government establishments. The fixed lens requires lower-middle-market firms supplying recurring or repeat outsourced services to external customers, so no in-code establishment can satisfy the firm and commercial-revenue conditions.
e.change_evidence: A formal NAICS revision that admits private operators to 922190, or evidence that the frozen population contains privately controlled in-code firms despite the official government-only definition.
caveats: The frozen n input is missing, consistent with the absence of SUSB private-firm coverage.
Private emergency-management and public-safety contractors exist but are classified in other NAICS industries and are outside this record.
Government corporations or quasi-public entities would require entity-level classification review but do not alter the stated industry definition.

s5.rationale: There are no eligible firms under the frozen lens, so a probability of qualifying private control transfer has no defined population. Government reorganizations, elections, interagency transfers, or leadership changes are not qualifying control transactions.
s5.change_evidence: Evidence of privately controlled firms legitimately classified in 922190 and a transaction history distinguishing control sales from public-sector reorganizations.
caveats: A numeric zero would imply an observed probability for an eligible denominator that does not exist.
Privatization or outsourcing of particular functions would move the activity to another industry rather than create an ordinary in-code firm transfer.

q.rationale: Commercial benefit retention after contract pass-through and repricing is undefined because in-code operators are government establishments funded by public budgets rather than firms charging external customers. Productivity benefits could reduce public cost or improve service capacity, but that is not the q construct.
q.change_evidence: A valid population of private, externally contracted 922190 firms with pricing terms and renewal histories, which would require resolving the conflict with the official government-only industry boundary.
caveats: Budget savings, avoided disaster losses, and improved public outcomes are economically valuable but cannot be translated into operator commercial retention under this lens.
Private technology vendors may retain contract benefits, but they belong to their own supplier industries.

d5.bridge: BLS projects employment for emergency management directors and GAO measures disaster frequency, costs, and federal workload rather than constant-price demand for the full 922190 public-service basket. I use the modest occupation outlook as a baseline and rising disaster complexity as upward pressure, while widening for public budgets, reclassification, shared roles, and policy changes; disaster counts can overstate staffing demand when technology or consolidation improves capacity.
d5.rationale: BLS expects emergency-management-director employment to grow and says preparedness needs sustain demand, though budget constraints can shift duties to other officials. GAO reports more costly and frequent disasters, a strained workforce, and hundreds of open declarations. These signals support durable, moderately growing public need with fiscal downside.
d5.change_evidence: Five-year federal, state, and local budget and staffing forecasts for 922190 functions, linked to hazard workload, disaster declarations, public-safety data mandates, grant volumes, and shared-service reorganizations.
caveats: Occupation demand is not identical to constant-quality public-service demand and includes some private-sector business-continuity work.
Disaster-assistance evidence emphasizes FEMA and recovery, while the NAICS code also includes other public-safety administration.
Appropriations and fiscal constraints can decouple service capacity from underlying public need.

o.rationale: Public-order, emergency-management, and safety-statistics functions require an accountable government authority to exercise statutory powers, coordinate agencies, approve plans, allocate resources, communicate with the public, and answer for high-stakes decisions. AI can automate work products but cannot replace the governmental operator within five years. Limited functions may be centralized or outsourced while accountability remains public.
o.change_evidence: Statutory or operational evidence that autonomous systems or private contractors can assume final public authority and accountability for emergency planning, public-safety administration, or official statistics without an in-code government operator.
caveats: This is a structural judgment rather than an observed displacement rate.
Shared services and intergovernmental consolidation may reduce establishment counts without eliminating accountable public operation.
Outsourcing task execution is not the same as eliminating governmental responsibility.

narrative.executive_view: The code contains strong AI-suitable administrative, planning, statistics, reporting, and information workflows, but it is structurally outside the roll-up lens: the official definition consists only of government establishments, with no commercial revenue denominator or defensible private-firm population. Productivity may matter to agencies, yet it cannot create an in-code acquisition opportunity.
narrative.ai_impact: AI can assist hazard research, scenario drafting, plan comparison, damage-assessment triage, public-safety statistics, grant documents, situation summaries, translation, communications, knowledge retrieval, and program reporting. Agency coordination, exercises, public communication, statutory decisions, operational command, and accountability remain human-led.
narrative.value_capture: Benefits would appear as faster service, more analytical capacity, reduced administrative burden, or lower public cost. They would accrue to taxpayers, agencies, constituents, and vendors through appropriations and procurement rather than to an operator retaining commercial margin after customer repricing.
narrative.firm_availability: There is no eligible in-code firm population: NAICS 922190 is explicitly limited to government establishments, and private contractors are classified elsewhere. Consequently the frozen missing n is substantive rather than merely a data-collection inconvenience, and qualifying control-transfer probability is undefined.
narrative.demand_durability: Emergency-planning and disaster-management need is durable and likely growing as disaster frequency, complexity, and open recovery workload strain public capacity. Budget constraints, shared roles, program consolidation, and political priorities can still limit measured staffing or spending despite rising underlying need.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: For AI implementation, major risks include fragmented legacy systems, sensitive public data, cybersecurity, bias, hallucinations, procurement delay, workforce shortages, records requirements, auditability, and failures during high-stakes events. For the screen, the decisive issue is not uncertainty but the government-only industry boundary.
narrative.principal_driver: Text- and data-heavy public-safety planning and reporting workflows offer meaningful agency productivity potential.
narrative.principal_weakness: Government-only establishments have no private control-transfer pool or commercial benefit-retention construct under the fixed lens.

sources.title: 2022 North American Industry Classification System Manual
sources.supports: Government-only industry boundary, included public-safety administration activities, and explicit exclusions.

sources.supports: Growth and stages of federal generative-AI use, emergency-management use categories, mission-support workflows, procurement, budget, staffing, bias, and reliability barriers.
